revision.text:
{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 太田命 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[上古#日本|上古]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = 太田 | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = | 氏族 = | 父母 = 父：[[子守尊]] | 兄弟 = | 配偶者 = | 子 = [[日向賀牟度美良姫]]、 | 特記事項 = }}
'''太田命'''（おおたのみこと）は、[[古代]]の人物、または[[神]]。[[猿田彦神]]の子孫。[[宇治土公]]の祖。[[神名秘書]]等には大田命とは[[興玉神]]の別称

{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 太田命 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[古墳時代]]、[[上古#日本|上古]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = 大田命、大田命神
一説：[[興玉神]]、[[大田田根子]] | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[垂仁天皇]]か | 氏族 = [[三輪氏]]か、宇治土公、浜名県主遠祖 | 父母 = | 兄弟 = | 配偶者 = | 子 = | 特記事項 = }} '''太田命'''（おおたのみこと）は、[[古代]]日本の人物。 == 概要 == 『皇太神宮儀式帳』では宇治土公（うじつちのきみ）の遠祖大田命とだけ記され、『大神宮諸雑事記』では宇治土公の遠祖で当地の[[土地神|土神]]とされている。「児島」系図では宇治土公の祖を[[久斯比賀多命]]三世孫の久斯気主命をとし、[[磯部氏|石部公]]（いそべのきみ）や狛人部（こまひとべ）と同族であるとされるから[[中田憲信]]「児島」『諸系譜』第二十六冊。、これに従えば大田命[[三輪氏]]の同族となる。 ところが、[[鎌倉時代]]成立と見られる『伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記』で突然「[[猿田彦|猿田彦大神]]は宇遅土公氏遠祖の神なり」と記されるようになり、『倭姫命世記』でも「猿田彦神の裔宇治土公氏の祖大田命」と主張するようになり、大田命の祖として猿田彦命が架上されたと指摘されている[[宝賀寿男]]「猿女君の意義　稗田阿禮の周辺（中）」『東アジアの古代文化』107号、2001年、174頁。。 『神名秘書』等には大田命とは[[興玉神]]の別称とされる。 濱名惣社神名宮は[[式内社]]の英多神社の論社で、浜名県主の祖神である太田命を祀り、[[大田田根子]]と同人とされるが、年代などを考えると実際には別人と見られる。また、これ関係する赤日子神社の境内社にも、太田命を祀る社口神社が存在する宝賀寿男「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/kaikokuso/kaikok2.htm 甲斐国造の系譜と一族の続き]」『古樹紀之房間』、2016年。。 『皇太神宮儀式帳』では、[[倭姫命]]が大田命に「汝の国の名はいかに」と問うと、「この河の名はさこくしる[[五十鈴川|伊須須の河]]」、「この河上はよき大宮地あり」と述べたと記される。 == 祀る神社 == *宇奈具志神社（[[新潟県]][[長岡市]]島崎） *[[須波阿湏疑神社]]（[[福井県]][[今立郡]][[池田町 (福井県)|池田町]]） *鵜甘神社（福井県今立郡池田町水海） *御穂神社 境内 稲荷神社（[[静岡県]][[静岡市]][[清水区]]三保） *濱名惣社神明宮 境内 太田命社（静岡県[[浜松市]][[北区 (浜松市)|北区]][[三ケ日町]]三ケ日大輪山） *赤日子神社 境内 社口神社（[[愛知県]][[蒲郡市]]神ノ郷町森） *川添神社（[[三重県]][[多気郡]][[大台町]]栃原） *岡神社（[[滋賀県]][[米原市]]間田） *[[津野神社 (高島市)|津野神社]] 境内 稲荷社（滋賀県[[高島市]][[今津町]]北仰） *門僕神社 境内 四社明社（[[奈良県]][[宇陀郡]][[曽爾村]]今井） *[[御杖神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（奈良県宇陀郡[[御杖村]]神末） *意多伎神社（[[島根県]][[安来市]]飯生町） *[[熊野大社]] 境内 伊邪那美神社（島根県[[松江市]][[八雲町 (松江市)|八雲町]]熊野） *胡簶神社（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]琴） == 関連項目 == *[[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] {{デフォルトソート:おおたのみこと}} [[Category:古代日本の人物]] [[Category:生年不明]] [[Category:没年不明]]

{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 太田命 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[古墳時代]]、[[上古#日本|上古]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = 大田命、大田命神
一説：[[興玉神]]、[[大田田根子]] | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[垂仁天皇]]か | 氏族 = [[三輪氏]]か、宇治土公、浜名県主遠祖 | 父母 = | 兄弟 = | 配偶者 = | 子 = | 特記事項 = }} '''太田命'''（おおたのみこと）は、[[古代]]日本の人物。 == 概要 == 『皇太神宮儀式帳』では宇治土公（うじつちのきみ）の遠祖大田命とだけ記され、『大神宮諸雑事記』では宇治土公の遠祖で当地の[[土地神|土神]]とされている。「児島」系図では宇治土公の祖を[[久斯比賀多命]]三世孫の久斯気主命をとし、[[磯部氏|石部公]]（いそべのきみ）や狛人部（こまひとべ）と同族であるとされるから[[中田憲信]]「児島」『諸系譜』第二十六冊。、これに従えば大田命[[三輪氏]]の同族となる。 ところが、[[鎌倉時代]]成立と見られる『伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記』で突然「[[猿田彦|猿田彦大神]]は宇遅土公氏遠祖の神なり」と記されるようになり、『倭姫命世記』でも「猿田彦神の裔宇治土公氏の祖大田命」と主張するようになり、大田命の祖として猿田彦命が架上されたと指摘されている[[宝賀寿男]]「猿女君の意義　稗田阿禮の周辺（中）」『東アジアの古代文化』107号、2001年、174頁。。 『神名秘書』等には大田命とは[[興玉神]]の別称とされる。 濱名惣社神名宮は[[式内社]]の英多神社の論社で、浜名県主の祖神である太田命を祀り、[[大田田根子]]と同人とされるが、年代などを考えると実際には別人と見られる。また、これ関係する赤日子神社の境内社にも、太田命を祀る社口神社が存在する宝賀寿男「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/kaikokuso/kaikok2.htm 甲斐国造の系譜と一族の続き]」『古樹紀之房間』、2016年。。 『皇太神宮儀式帳』では、[[倭姫命]]が大田命に「汝の国の名はいかに」と問うと、「この河の名はさこくしる[[五十鈴川|伊須須の河]]」、「この河上はよき大宮地あり」と述べたと記される。 == 祀る神社 == *[[鼻節神社]]（[[宮城県]][[宮城郡]][[七ヶ浜町]]花淵浜字誰道）祭神に数える説あり *宇奈具志神社（[[新潟県]][[長岡市]]島崎） *[[須波阿湏疑神社]]（[[福井県]][[今立郡]][[池田町 (福井県)|池田町]]） *鵜甘神社（福井県今立郡池田町水海） *[[筑波山神社]] 境内 朝日稲荷神社（[[茨城県]][[つくば市]][[筑波 (つくば市)|筑波]]） *[[杉山神社 (横浜市緑区西八朔町)|杉山神社]]（[[神奈川県]][[横浜市]][[緑区 (横浜市)|緑区]][[西八朔町]]） *[[御穂神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（[[静岡県]][[静岡市]][[清水区]]三保） *[[高室神社]]（静岡県[[掛川市]][[大渕 (掛川市)|大渕]]） *濱名惣社神明宮 境内 太田命社（静岡県[[浜松市]][[北区 (浜松市)|北区]][[三ケ日町]]三ケ日大輪山） *[[塩釜神社 (豊橋市)|塩釜神社]]（[[愛知県]][[豊橋市]]大山町字東大山） *赤日子神社 境内 社口神社（愛知県[[蒲郡市]]神ノ郷町森） *川添神社（[[三重県]][[多気郡]][[大台町]]栃原） *岡神社（[[滋賀県]][[米原市]]間田） *[[津野神社 (高島市)|津野神社]] 境内 稲荷社（滋賀県[[高島市]][[今津町]]北仰） *門僕神社 境内 四社明社（[[奈良県]][[宇陀郡]][[曽爾村]]今井） *[[御杖神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（奈良県宇陀郡[[御杖村]]神末） *[[須佐神社 (田辺市)|須佐神社]] 境内 厳島神社（[[和歌山県]][[田辺市]]中万呂） *[[石部神社 (豊岡市)|石部神社]] 境内 皇大神宮（[[兵庫県]][[豊岡市]][[出石町]]下谷） *意多伎神社（[[島根県]][[安来市]]飯生町） *[[熊野大社]] 境内 伊邪那美神社（島根県[[松江市]][[八雲町 (松江市)|八雲町]]熊野） *胡簶神社（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]琴 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == *[[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] *[[猿田彦神社]] {{デフォルトソート:おおたのみこと}} [[Category:古代日本の人物]] [[Category:生年不明]] [[Category:没年不明]]

濱名惣社神名宮は[[式内社]]の英多神社の論社で、浜名県主の祖神である太田命を祀り、[[大田田根子]]と同人とされるが、年代などを考えると実際には別人と見られる。また、これ関係する赤日子神社の境内社にも、太田命を祀る社口神社が存在する宝賀寿男「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/kaikokuso/kaikok2.htm 甲斐国造の系譜と一族の続き]」『古樹紀之房間』、2016年。。 『皇太神宮儀式帳』では、[[倭姫命]]が大田命に「汝の国の名はいかに」と問うと、「この河の名はさこくしる[[五十鈴川|伊須須の河]]」、「この河上はよき大宮地あり」と述べたと記される。 == 祀る神社 == *[[嶽六所神社]]（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]][[神宮寺 (大仙市)|神宮寺]]字落貝） *[[鼻節神社]]（[[宮城県]][[宮城郡]][[七ヶ浜町]]花淵浜字誰道）祭神に数える説あり *宇奈具志神社（[[新潟県]][[長岡市]]島崎） *[[須波阿湏疑神社]]（[[福井県]][[今立郡]][[池田町 (福井県)|池田町]]） *鵜甘神社（福井県今立郡池田町水海） *[[筑波山神社]] 境内 朝日稲荷神社（[[茨城県]][[つくば市]][[筑波 (つくば市)|筑波]]） *[[冠稲荷神社]]（[[群馬県]][[太田市]]細谷町） *[[北谷稲荷神社]]（[[東京都]][[渋谷区]][[神南#神南一丁目|神南一丁目]]） *[[杉山神社 (横浜市緑区西八朔町)|杉山神社]]（[[神奈川県]][[横浜市]][[緑区 (横浜市)|緑区]][[西八朔町]]） *洲崎神社（神奈川県横浜市[[金沢区]][[洲崎町]]） *五社稲荷神社（神奈川県[[鎌倉市]][[岩瀬 (鎌倉市)|岩瀬]]） *猿田彦神社（[[長野県]][[松本市]][[宮渕 (松本市)|宮渕]]） *[[御穂神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（[[静岡県]][[静岡市]][[清水区]]三保） *[[高室神社]]（静岡県[[掛川市]][[大渕 (掛川市)|大渕]]） *濱名惣社神明宮 境内 太田命社（静岡県[[浜松市]][[北区 (浜松市)|北区]][[三ケ日町]]三ケ日大輪山） *[[塩釜神社 (豊橋市)|塩釜神社]]（[[愛知県]][[豊橋市]]大山町字東大山） *赤日子神社 境内 社口神社（愛知県[[蒲郡市]]神ノ郷町森） *[[洲崎濱宮神明神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（[[三重県]][[四日市市]][[塩浜地区]]） *川添神社（三重県[[多気郡]][[大台町]]栃原） *岡神社（[[滋賀県]][[米原市]]間田） *太田神社（滋賀県[[高島市]][[安曇川町]]青柳） *[[津野神社 (高島市)|津野神社]] 境内 稲荷社（滋賀県高島市[[今津町]]北仰） *門僕神社 境内 四社明社（[[奈良県]][[宇陀郡]][[曽爾村]]今井） *[[御杖神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（奈良県宇陀郡[[御杖村]]神末） *[[地主神社]]（[[京都府]][[京都市]][[東山区]]清水） *[[大虫神社 (与謝野町)|大虫神社]]（京都府[[与謝郡]][[与謝野町]]温江字虫本） *[[須佐神社 (田辺市)|須佐神社]] 境内 厳島神社（[[和歌山県]][[田辺市]]中万呂） *二村神社（[[兵庫県]][[丹波篠山市]]味間奥） *[[石部神社 (豊岡市)|石部神社]] 境内 皇大神宮（兵庫県[[豊岡市]][[出石町]]下谷） *意多伎神社（[[島根県]][[安来市]]飯生町） *[[熊野大社]] 境内 伊邪那美神社（島根県[[松江市]][[八雲町 (松江市)|八雲町]]熊野） *胡簶神社（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]琴） *岩崎神社（[[大分県]][[宇佐市]]岩崎） == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == *[[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] *[[猿田彦神社]] {{デフォルトソート:おおたのみこと}} [[Category:古代日本の人物]] [[Category:生年不明]] [[Category:没年不明]]

ところが、[[鎌倉時代]]成立と見られる『伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記』で突然「[[猿田彦|猿田彦大神]]は宇遅土公氏遠祖の神なり」と記されるようになり、『倭姫命世記』でも「猿田彦神の裔宇治土公氏の祖大田命」と主張するようになり、大田命の祖として猿田彦命が架上されたと指摘されている[[宝賀寿男]]「猿女君の意義　稗田阿禮の周辺（中）」『東アジアの古代文化』107号、2001年、174頁。。 濱名惣社神名宮は[[式内社]]の英多神社の論社で、浜名県主の祖神である太田命を祀り、[[大田田根子]]と同人とされるが、年代などを考えると実際には別人と見られる。また、これ関係する赤日子神社の境内社にも、太田命を祀る社口神社が存在する宝賀寿男「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/kaikokuso/kaikok2.htm 甲斐国造の系譜と一族の続き]」『古樹紀之房間』、2016年。。 『皇太神宮儀式帳』では、[[倭姫命]]が大田命に「汝の国の名はいかに」と問うと、「この河の名はさこくしる[[五十鈴川|伊須須の河]]」、「この河上はよき大宮地あり」と述べたと記される。 == 祀る神社 == *[[嶽六所神社]]（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]][[神宮寺 (大仙市)|神宮寺]]字落貝） {{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 太田命 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[古墳時代]]、[[上古#日本|上古]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = 大田命、大田命神
一説：[[興玉神]]、[[大田田根子]] | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[垂仁天皇]]か | 氏族 = [[三輪氏]]か、宇治土公、浜名県主遠祖 | 父母 = | 兄弟 = | 配偶者 = | 子 = | 特記事項 = }} '''太田命'''（おおたのみこと）は、[[古代]]日本の人物。 == 概要 == 『皇太神宮儀式帳』では宇治土公（うじつちのきみ）の遠祖大田命とだけ記され、『大神宮諸雑事記』では宇治土公の遠祖で当地の[[土地神|土神]]とされている。「児島」系図では宇治土公の祖を[[久斯比賀多命]]三世孫の久斯気主命をとし、[[磯部氏|石辺公]]（いそべのきみ）や狛人部（こまひとべ）と同族であるとされるから[[中田憲信]]「児島」『諸系譜』第二十六冊。、これに従えば大田命は[[三輪氏]]の同族となる。 ところが、[[鎌倉時代]]成立と見られる『伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記』で突然「[[猿田彦|猿田彦大神]]は宇遅土公氏遠祖の神なり」と記されるようになり、『倭姫命世記』でも「猿田彦神の裔宇治土公氏の祖大田命」と主張するようになり、大田命の祖として猿田彦命が架上されたと指摘されている[[宝賀寿男]]「猿女君の意義　稗田阿禮の周辺（中）」『東アジアの古代文化』107号、2001年、174頁。。 『神名秘書』等には大田命とは[[興玉神]]の別称とされる。 濱名惣社神名宮は[[式内社]]の英多神社の論社で、浜名県主の祖神である太田命を祀り、[[大田田根子]]と同人とされるが、年代などを考えると実際には別人と見られる。また、これ関係する赤日子神社の境内社にも、太田命を祀る社口神社が存在する宝賀寿男「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/kaikokuso/kaikok2.htm 甲斐国造の系譜と一族の続き]」『古樹紀之房間』、2016年。。 『皇太神宮儀式帳』では、[[倭姫命]]が大田命に「汝の国の名はいかに」と問うと、「この河の名はさこくしる[[五十鈴川|伊須須の河]]」、「この河上はよき大宮地あり」と述べたと記される。 == 祀る神社 == *[[嶽六所神社]]（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]][[神宮寺 (大仙市)|神宮寺]]字落貝） *[[鼻節神社]]（[[宮城県]][[宮城郡]][[七ヶ浜町]]花淵浜字誰道）祭神に数える説あり *宇奈具志神社（[[新潟県]][[長岡市]]島崎） *[[須波阿湏疑神社]]（[[福井県]][[今立郡]][[池田町 (福井県)|池田町]]） *鵜甘神社（福井県今立郡池田町水海） *[[筑波山神社]] 境内 朝日稲荷神社（[[茨城県]][[つくば市]][[筑波 (つくば市)|筑波]]） *[[冠稲荷神社]]（[[群馬県]][[太田市]]細谷町） *[[北谷稲荷神社]]（[[東京都]][[渋谷区]][[神南#神南一丁目|神南一丁目]]） *[[杉山神社 (横浜市緑区西八朔町)|杉山神社]]（[[神奈川県]][[横浜市]][[緑区 (横浜市)|緑区]][[西八朔町]]） *洲崎神社（神奈川県横浜市[[金沢区]][[洲崎町]]） *五社稲荷神社（神奈川県[[鎌倉市]][[岩瀬 (鎌倉市)|岩瀬]]） *猿田彦神社（[[長野県]][[松本市]][[宮渕 (松本市)|宮渕]]） *[[御穂神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（[[静岡県]][[静岡市]][[清水区]]三保） *[[高室神社]]（静岡県[[掛川市]][[大渕 (掛川市)|大渕]]） *濱名惣社神明宮 境内 太田命社（静岡県[[浜松市]][[北区 (浜松市)|北区]][[三ケ日町]]三ケ日大輪山） *[[塩釜神社 (豊橋市)|塩釜神社]]（[[愛知県]][[豊橋市]]大山町字東大山） *赤日子神社 境内 社口神社（愛知県[[蒲郡市]]神ノ郷町森） *[[洲崎濱宮神明神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（[[三重県]][[四日市市]][[塩浜地区]]） *川添神社（三重県[[多気郡]][[大台町]]栃原） *岡神社（[[滋賀県]][[米原市]]間田） *太田神社（滋賀県[[高島市]][[安曇川町]]青柳） *[[津野神社 (高島市)|津野神社]] 境内 稲荷社（滋賀県高島市[[今津町 (滋賀県)|今津町]]北仰） *門僕神社 境内 四社明社（[[奈良県]][[宇陀郡]][[曽爾村]]今井） *[[御杖神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（奈良県宇陀郡[[御杖村]]神末） *[[地主神社]]（[[京都府]][[京都市]][[東山区]]清水） *[[大虫神社 境内 伊邪那美神社（島根県[[松江市]][[八雲町 (松江市)|八雲町]]熊野） *胡簶神社（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]琴） *岩崎神社（[[大分県]][[宇佐市]]岩崎） == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == *[[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] *[[猿田彦神社]] {{デフォルトソート:おおたのみこと}} [[Category:古代日本の人物]] {{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 太田命 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[古墳時代]]、[[上古#日本|上古]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = 大田命、大田命神
一説：[[興玉神]]、[[大田田根子]] | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[垂仁天皇]]か | 氏族 = [[三輪氏]]族、宇治土公、狛人野（狛人部）、[[磯部氏|石辺公]]、浜名県主祖 | 父母 = 父：甕多須耳命[[中田憲信]]『神別系譜』、[[東京国立博物館]]デジタルライブラリー。 | 兄弟 = | 配偶者 = | 子 = | 特記事項 = 称[[猿田彦神]]苗裔 }} '''太田命'''（おおたのみこと）は、[[古代]]日本の人物。 == 概要 == 『皇太神宮儀式帳』では宇治土公（うじつちのきみ）の遠祖大田命とだけ記され、『大神宮諸雑事記』では宇治土公の遠祖で当地の[[土地神|土神]]とされている。「児島」系図では宇治土公の祖を[[久斯比賀多命]]三世孫の久斯気主命をとし、[[磯部氏|石辺公]]（いそべのきみ）や狛人部（こまひとべ）と同族であるとされ中田憲信「児島」『諸系譜』第二十六冊。、『神別系譜』では太田命が久斯気主命の三世孫とされることから、太田命は[[三輪氏]]の同族となる。 ところが、[[鎌倉時代]]成立と見られる『伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記』で突然「[[猿田彦|猿田彦大神]]は宇遅土公氏遠祖の神なり」と記されるようになり、『倭姫命世記』でも「猿田彦神の裔宇治土公氏の祖大田命」と主張するようになり、大田命の祖として猿田彦命が架上されたと指摘されている[[宝賀寿男]]「猿女君の意義　稗田阿禮の周辺（中）」『東アジアの古代文化』107号、2001年、174頁。。 『神名秘書』等には大田命とは[[興玉神]]の別称とされる。 濱名惣社神名宮は[[式内社]]の英多神社の論社で、浜名県主の祖神である太田命を祀り、[[大田田根子]]と同人とされるが、年代などを考えると実際には別人と見られる。また、これ関係する赤日子神社の境内社にも、太田命を祀る社口神社が存在する宝賀寿男「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/kaikokuso/kaikok2.htm 甲斐国造の系譜と一族の続き]」『古樹紀之房間』、2016年。。 『皇太神宮儀式帳』では、[[倭姫命]]が大田命に「汝の国の名はいかに」と問うと、「この河の名はさこくしる[[五十鈴川|伊須須の河]]」、「この河上はよき大宮地あり」と述べたと記される。 == 祀る神社 == *[[嶽六所神社]]（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]][[神宮寺 (大仙市)|神宮寺]]字落貝） *[[鼻節神社]]（[[宮城県]][[宮城郡]][[七ヶ浜町]]花淵浜字誰道）祭神に数える説あり *宇奈具志神社（[[新潟県]][[長岡市]]島崎） *[[須波阿湏疑神社]]（[[福井県]][[今立郡]][[池田町 (福井県)|池田町]]） *鵜甘神社（福井県今立郡池田町水海） *[[筑波山神社]] 境内 朝日稲荷神社（[[茨城県]][[つくば市]][[筑波 (つくば市)|筑波]]） *[[冠稲荷神社]]（[[群馬県]][[太田市]]細谷町） *[[北谷稲荷神社]]（[[東京都]][[渋谷区]][[神南#神南一丁目|神南一丁目]]） *[[杉山神社 (横浜市緑区西八朔町)|杉山神社]]（[[神奈川県]][[横浜市]][[緑区 (横浜市)|緑区]][[西八朔町]]） *洲崎神社（神奈川県横浜市[[金沢区]][[洲崎町]]） *五社稲荷神社（神奈川県[[鎌倉市]][[岩瀬 (鎌倉市)|岩瀬]]） *猿田彦神社（[[長野県]][[松本市]][[宮渕 (松本市)|宮渕]]） *[[御穂神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（[[静岡県]][[静岡市]][[清水区]]三保） *[[高室神社]]（静岡県[[掛川市]][[大渕 (掛川市)|大渕]]） *濱名惣社神明宮 境内 太田命社（静岡県[[浜松市]][[北区 (浜松市)|北区]][[三ケ日町]]三ケ日大輪山） *[[塩釜神社 境内 伊邪那美神社（島根県[[松江市]][[八雲町 (松江市)|八雲町]]熊野） *胡簶神社（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]琴） *岩崎神社（[[大分県]][[宇佐市]]岩崎） == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == *[[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] *[[猿田彦神社]] {{デフォルトソート:おおたのみこと}} [[Category:古代日本の人物]] '''太田命'''（おおたのみこと）は、[[古代]]日本の人物。
== 概要 == 『皇太神宮儀式帳』では宇治土公（うじつちのきみ）の遠祖大田命とだけ記され、『大神宮諸雑事記』では宇治土公の遠祖で当地の[[土地神|土神]]とされている。「児島」系図では宇治土公の祖を[[久斯比賀多命]]三世孫の久斯気主命をとし、[[磯部氏|石辺公]]（いそべのきみ）や狛人部（こまひとべ）と同族であるとされ<ref>中田憲信「児島」『諸系譜』第二十六冊。</ref>、『神別系譜』では太田命が久斯気主命の三世孫とされることから<ref name="keifu"/>、太田命は[[三輪氏]]の同族となる。
ところが、[[鎌倉時代]]成立と見られる『伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記』で突然「[[猿田彦|猿田彦大神]]は宇遅土公氏遠祖の神なり」と記されるようになり、『倭姫命世記』でも「猿田彦神の裔宇治土公氏の祖大田命」と主張するようになり、大田命の祖として猿田彦命が架上されたと指摘されている<ref>[[宝賀寿男]]「猿女君の意義 稗田阿禮の周辺（中）」『東アジアの古代文化』107号、2001年、174頁。</ref>。
『神名秘書』等には大田命とは[[興玉神]]の別称とされる。
濱名惣社神名宮は[[式内社]]の英多神社の論社で、浜名県主の祖神である太田命を祀り、[[大田田根子]]と同人とされるが、年代などを考えると実際には別人と見られる。また、これ関係する赤日子神社の境内社にも、太田命を祀る社口神社が存在する<ref>宝賀寿男「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/kaikokuso/kaikok2.htm 甲斐国造の系譜と一族の続き]」『古樹紀之房間』、2016年。</ref>。
『皇太神宮儀式帳』では、[[倭姫命]]が大田命に「汝の国の名はいかに」と問うと、「この河の名はさこくしる[[五十鈴川|伊須須の河]]」、「この河上はよき大宮地あり」と述べたと記される。
== 祀る神社 == *[[嶽六所神社]]（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]][[神宮寺 (大仙市)|神宮寺]]字落貝） *[[鼻節神社]]（[[宮城県]][[宮城郡]][[七ヶ浜町]]花淵浜字誰道）祭神に数える説あり *宇奈具志神社（[[新潟県]][[長岡市]]島崎） *[[須波阿湏疑神社]]（[[福井県]][[今立郡]][[池田町 (福井県)|池田町]]） *鵜甘神社（福井県今立郡池田町水海） *[[筑波山神社]] 境内 朝日稲荷神社（[[茨城県]][[つくば市]][[筑波 (つくば市)|筑波]]） *[[冠稲荷神社]]（[[群馬県]][[太田市]]細谷町） *[[北谷稲荷神社]]（[[東京都]][[渋谷区]][[神南#神南一丁目|神南一丁目]]） *[[杉山神社 (横浜市緑区西八朔町)|杉山神社]]（[[神奈川県]][[横浜市]][[緑区 (横浜市)|緑区]][[西八朔町]]） *洲崎神社（神奈川県横浜市[[金沢区]][[洲崎町]]） *五社稲荷神社（神奈川県[[鎌倉市]][[岩瀬 (鎌倉市)|岩瀬]]） *猿田彦神社（[[長野県]][[松本市]][[宮渕 (松本市)|宮渕]]） *[[御穂神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（[[静岡県]][[静岡市]][[清水区]]三保） *[[高室神社]]（静岡県[[掛川市]][[大渕 (掛川市)|大渕]]） *濱名惣社神明宮 境内 太田命社（静岡県[[浜松市]][[北区 (浜松市)|北区]][[三ケ日町]]三ケ日大輪山） *[[塩釜神社 (豊橋市)|塩釜神社]]（[[愛知県]][[豊橋市]]大山町字東大山） *赤日子神社 境内 社口神社（愛知県[[蒲郡市]]神ノ郷町森） *[[洲崎濱宮神明神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（[[三重県]][[四日市市]][[塩浜地区]]） *川添神社（三重県[[多気郡]][[大台町]]栃原） *岡神社（[[滋賀県]][[米原市]]間田） *太田神社（滋賀県[[高島市]][[安曇川町]]青柳） *[[津野神社 (高島市)|津野神社]] 境内 稲荷社（滋賀県高島市[[今津町 (滋賀県)|今津町]]北仰） *門僕神社 境内 四社明社（[[奈良県]][[宇陀郡]][[曽爾村]]今井） *[[御杖神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（奈良県宇陀郡[[御杖村]]神末） *[[地主神社]]（[[京都府]][[京都市]][[東山区]]清水） *[[大虫神社 (与謝野町)|大虫神社]]（京都府[[与謝郡]][[与謝野町]]温江字虫本） *[[須佐神社 (田辺市)|須佐神社]] 境内 厳島神社（[[和歌山県]][[田辺市]]中万呂） *二村神社（[[兵庫県]][[丹波篠山市]]味間奥） *[[石部神社 (豊岡市)|石部神社]] 境内 皇大神宮（兵庫県[[豊岡市]][[出石町]]下谷） *意多伎神社（[[島根県]][[安来市]]飯生町） *[[熊野大社]] 境内 伊邪那美神社（島根県[[松江市]][[八雲町 (松江市)|八雲町]]熊野） *胡簶神社（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]琴） *岩崎神社（[[大分県]][[宇佐市]]岩崎）

{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 太田命 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[古墳時代]]、[[上古#日本|上古]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = 大田命 | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = | 氏族 = 宇治土公 | 父母 = | 兄弟 = | 配偶者 = | 子 = | 特記事項 =[[猿田彦神]]苗裔 }}
'''太田命'''（おおたのみこと）は、[[古代]]日本の人物。
== 概要 == 『皇太神宮儀式帳』では宇治土公（うじつちのきみ）の遠祖大田命とだけ記され、『大神宮諸雑事記』では宇治土公の遠祖で当地の[[土地神|土神]]とされている。
[[鎌倉時代]]成立と見られる『伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記』では「[[猿田彦|猿田彦大神]]は宇遅土公氏遠祖の神なり」と記されるようになり、『倭姫命世記』でも「猿田彦神の裔宇治土公氏の祖大田命」と主張するようになり、大田命の祖として猿田彦命の名前が登場するようになった。
『神名秘書』等には大田命とは[[興玉神]]の別称とされる。
濱名惣社神名宮は[[式内社]]の英多神社の論社で、浜名県主の祖神である太田命を祀る。
『皇太神宮儀式帳』では、[[倭姫命]]が大田命に「汝の国の名はいかに」と問うと、「この河の名はさこくしる[[五十鈴川|伊須須の河]]」、「この河上はよき大宮地あり」と述べたと記される。
== 祀る神社 == *[[嶽六所神社]]（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]][[神宮寺 (大仙市)|神宮寺]]字落貝） *[[鼻節神社]]（[[宮城県]][[宮城郡]][[七ヶ浜町]]花淵浜字誰道）祭神に数える説あり *宇奈具志神社（[[新潟県]][[長岡市]]島崎） *[[須波阿湏疑神社]]（[[福井県]][[今立郡]][[池田町 (福井県)|池田町]]） *鵜甘神社（福井県今立郡池田町水海） *[[筑波山神社]] 境内 朝日稲荷神社（[[茨城県]][[つくば市]][[筑波 (つくば市)|筑波]]） *[[冠稲荷神社]]（[[群馬県]][[太田市]]細谷町） *[[北谷稲荷神社]]（[[東京都]][[渋谷区]][[神南#神南一丁目|神南一丁目]]） *[[杉山神社 (横浜市緑区西八朔町)|杉山神社]]（[[神奈川県]][[横浜市]][[緑区 (横浜市)|緑区]][[西八朔町]]） *洲崎神社（神奈川県横浜市[[金沢区]][[洲崎町]]） *五社稲荷神社（神奈川県[[鎌倉市]][[岩瀬 (鎌倉市)|岩瀬]]） *猿田彦神社（[[長野県]][[松本市]][[宮渕 (松本市)|宮渕]]） *[[御穂神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（[[静岡県]][[静岡市]][[清水区]]三保） *[[高室神社]]（静岡県[[掛川市]][[大渕 (掛川市)|大渕]]） *濱名惣社神明宮 境内 太田命社（静岡県[[浜松市]][[北区 (浜松市)|北区]][[三ケ日町]]三ケ日大輪山） *[[塩釜神社 (豊橋市)|塩釜神社]]（[[愛知県]][[豊橋市]]大山町字東大山） *赤日子神社 境内 社口神社（愛知県[[蒲郡市]]神ノ郷町森） *[[洲崎濱宮神明神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（[[三重県]][[四日市市]][[塩浜地区]]） *川添神社（三重県[[多気郡]][[大台町]]栃原） *岡神社（[[滋賀県]][[米原市]]間田） *太田神社（滋賀県[[高島市]][[安曇川町]]青柳） *[[津野神社 (高島市)|津野神社]] 境内 稲荷社（滋賀県高島市[[今津町 (滋賀県)|今津町]]北仰） *門僕神社 境内 四社明社（[[奈良県]][[宇陀郡]][[曽爾村]]今井） *[[御杖神社]] 境内 稲荷神社（奈良県宇陀郡[[御杖村]]神末） *[[地主神社]]（[[京都府]][[京都市]][[東山区]]清水） *[[大虫神社 (与謝野町)|大虫神社]]（京都府[[与謝郡]][[与謝野町]]温江字虫本） *[[須佐神社 (田辺市)|須佐神社]] 境内 厳島神社（[[和歌山県]][[田辺市]]中万呂） *二村神社（[[兵庫県]][[丹波篠山市]]味間奥） *[[石部神社 (豊岡市)|石部神社]] 境内 皇大神宮（兵庫県[[豊岡市]][[出石町]]下谷） *意多伎神社（[[島根県]][[安来市]]飯生町） *[[熊野大社]] 境内 伊邪那美神社（島根県[[松江市]][[八雲町 (松江市)|八雲町]]熊野） *胡簶神社（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]琴） *岩崎神社（[[大分県]][[宇佐市]]岩崎）

revision.text:
Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />

==Overview==
{{Infobox Japanese noble | name = Ōta no Mikoto (太田命) | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|上古}} | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = | other_names = Ōta no Mikoto (大田命) | posthumous_name = | divine_name = | dharma_name = | burial_place = | court_rank = | lord = | clan = {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}} | parents = | siblings = | spouse = | children = | notable_remarks = Descendant of {{ill|Sarutahiko-no-Kami|ja|猿田彦神}} }}
'''Ōta no Mikoto''' (太田命) was a figure in ancient Japan.
== Overview ==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==

He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

revision.text:
Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==

Inari Shrine|ja|冠稲荷神社}} (Gunma Prefecture, Ōta City) * {{ill|Kitadani Inari Shrine|ja|北谷稲荷神社}} (Tokyo, Shibuya Ward) * {{ill|Sugiyama Shrine|ja|杉山神社 (横浜市緑区西八朔町)}} (Kanagawa Prefecture, Yokohama City) * Susaki Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture, Yokohama City) * {{ill|Gosha Inari Shrine|ja|五社稲荷神社}} (Kanagawa Prefecture, Kamakura City) * {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} (Nagano Prefecture, Matsumoto City) * {{ill|Miho Shrine|ja|御穂神社}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Shizuoka City) * Takamuro Shrine (Shizuoka Prefecture, Kakegawa City) * Hamanasoja Shrine (Shizuoka Prefecture, Hamamatsu City) * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|塩釜神社 (豊橋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Toyohashi City) * Akahiko Shrine (Aichi Prefecture, Gamagōri City) * {{ill|Susakihama Shrine|ja|洲崎濱宮神明神社}} (Mie Prefecture, Yokkaichi City) * Kawazoe Shrine (Mie Prefecture, Ōdai Town) * Oka Shrine (Shiga Prefecture, Maibara City) * Ōta Shrine (Shiga Prefecture, Takashima City) * {{ill|Tsuno Shrine|ja|津野神社}} (Shiga Prefecture, Takashima City) * Monboku Shrine (Nara Prefecture, Soni Village) * {{ill|Mitsu Shrine|ja|御杖神社}} (Nara Prefecture, Mitsue Village) * {{ill|Jishu Shrine|ja|地主神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City) * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)}} (Kyoto Prefecture, Yosano Town) * {{ill|Susa Shrine|ja|須佐神社 (田辺市)}} (Wakayama Prefecture, Tanabe City) * Futamura Shrine (Hyōgo Prefecture, Tamba Sasayama City) * {{ill|Ishibe revision.comment: Adding [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==

Inari Shrine|ja|冠稲荷神社}} (Gunma Prefecture, Ōta City) * {{ill|Kitadani Inari Shrine|ja|北谷稲荷神社}} (Tokyo, Shibuya Ward) * {{ill|Sugiyama Shrine|ja|杉山神社 (横浜市緑区西八朔町)}} (Kanagawa Prefecture, Yokohama City) * Susaki Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture, Yokohama City) * {{ill|Gosha Inari Shrine|ja|五社稲荷神社}} (Kanagawa Prefecture, Kamakura City) * {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} (Nagano Prefecture, Matsumoto {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|塩釜神社 (豊橋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Toyohashi City) * Akahiko Shrine (Aichi Prefecture, Gamagōri City) * {{ill|Susakihama Shrine|ja|洲崎濱宮神明神社}} (Mie Prefecture, Yokkaichi City) * Kawazoe Shrine (Mie Prefecture, Ōdai Town) * Oka Shrine (Shiga Prefecture, Maibara City) * Ōta Shrine (Shiga Prefecture, Takashima City) * {{ill|Tsuno Shrine|ja|津野神社}} (Shiga Prefecture, Takashima City) * Monboku Shrine (Nara Prefecture, Soni Village) * {{ill|Mitsu Shrine|ja|御杖神社}} (Nara Prefecture, Mitsue Village) * {{ill|Jishu Shrine|ja|地主神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City) * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)}} (Kyoto Prefecture, Yosano Town) * {{ill|Susa Shrine|ja|須佐神社 (田辺市)}} (Wakayama Prefecture, Tanabe City) * Futamura Shrine (Hyōgo Prefecture, Tamba Sasayama City) * {{ill|Ishibe Shrine|ja|石部神社 (豊岡市)}} (Hyōgo Prefecture, Toyooka City) * Itaki Shrine (Shimane Prefecture, Yasugi City) * {{ill|Kumano Taisha|ja|熊野大社}} (Shimane Prefecture, Matsue City) * Koroku Shrine (Nagasaki Prefecture, Tsushima City) * Iwasaki Shrine (Ōita Prefecture, Usa City) * Ninomiya Shrine (Fukuoka Prefecture, Fukuoka City)

revision.text:
Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

Inari Shrine|ja|冠稲荷神社}} (Gunma Prefecture, Ōta City) * {{ill|Kitadani Inari Shrine|ja|北谷稲荷神社}} (Tokyo, Shibuya Ward) * {{ill|Sugiyama Shrine|ja|杉山神社 (横浜市緑区西八朔町)}} (Kanagawa Prefecture, Yokohama City) * Susaki Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture, Yokohama City) * {{ill|Gosha Inari Shrine|ja|五社稲荷神社}} (Kanagawa Prefecture, Kamakura City) * {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} (Nagano Prefecture, Matsumoto Shrine|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)}} (Kyoto Prefecture, Yosano Town) * {{ill|Susa Shrine|ja|須佐神社 (田辺市)}} (Wakayama Prefecture, Tanabe City) * Futamura Shrine (Hyōgo Prefecture, Tamba Sasayama City) * {{ill|Ishibe Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

revision.text:
Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[category{{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

revision.text:
Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==

City) * {{ill|Miho Shrine|ja|御穂神社}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Shizuoka City) * Takamuro Shrine (Shizuoka Prefecture, Kakegawa City) * Hamanasoja Shrine (Shizuoka Prefecture, Hamamatsu City) * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|塩釜神社 (豊橋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Toyohashi City) * Akahiko Shrine (Aichi Prefecture, Gamagōri City) * {{ill|Susakihama Shrine|ja|洲崎濱宮神明神社}} (Mie Prefecture, Yokkaichi City) * Kawazoe Shrine (Mie Prefecture, Ōdai Town) * Oka Shrine (Shiga Prefecture, Maibara City) * Ōta Shrine (Shiga Prefecture, Takashima City) * {{ill|Tsuno Shrine|ja|津野神社}} (Shiga Prefecture, Takashima City) * Monboku Shrine (Nara Prefecture, Soni Village) * {{ill|Mitsu Shrine|ja|御杖神社}} (Nara Prefecture, Mitsue Village) * {{ill|Jishu Shrine|ja|地主神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City) * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)}} (Kyoto Prefecture, Yosano Town) * {{ill|Susa Shrine|ja|須佐神社 (田辺市)}} (Wakayama Prefecture, Tanabe City) * Futamura Shrine (Hyōgo Prefecture, Tamba Sasayama City) * {{ill|Ishibe Shrine|ja|石部神社 (豊岡市)}} (Hyōgo Prefecture, Toyooka City) * Itaki Shrine (Shimane Prefecture, Yasugi City) * {{ill|Kumano Taisha|ja|熊野大社}} (Shimane Prefecture, Matsue City) * Koroku Shrine (Nagasaki Prefecture, Tsushima City) * Iwasaki Shrine (Ōita Prefecture, Usa City) * Ninomiya Shrine (Fukuoka Prefecture, Fukuoka City)

By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.

Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.

From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.

He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==

revision.text:
Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:{{subst:PAGENAME}}}}[[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==
* {{ill|Take Rokusho Shrine|ja|嶽六所神社}} (Akita Prefecture, Daisen City) * {{ill|Hanabue Shrine|ja|鼻節神社}} (Miyagi Prefecture, Miyagi District) * Unagushi Shrine (Niigata Prefecture, Nagaoka City) * {{ill|Sunahaasu Shrine|ja|須波阿湏疑神社}} (Fukui Prefecture, Ikeda Town) * Ugama Shrine (Fukui Prefecture, Ikeda Town) * {{ill|Tsukubasan Shrine|ja|筑波山神社}} (Ibaraki Prefecture, Tsukuba City) * {{ill|Kanmuri Inari Shrine|ja|冠稲荷神社}} (Gunma Prefecture, Ōta City) * {{ill|Kitadani Inari Shrine|ja|北谷稲荷神社}} (Tokyo, Shibuya Ward) * {{ill|Sugiyama Shrine|ja|杉山神社 (横浜市緑区西八朔町)}} (Kanagawa Prefecture, Yokohama City) * Susaki Shrine (Kanagawa Prefecture, Yokohama City) * {{ill|Gosha Inari Shrine|ja|五社稲荷神社}} (Kanagawa Prefecture, Kamakura City) * {{ill|Sarutahiko Shrine|ja|猿田彦神社}} (Nagano Prefecture, Matsumoto City) * {{ill|Miho Shrine|ja|御穂神社}} (Shizuoka Prefecture, Shizuoka City) * Takamuro Shrine (Shizuoka Prefecture, Kakegawa City) * Hamanasoja Shrine (Shizuoka Prefecture, Hamamatsu City) * {{ill|Shiogama Shrine|ja|塩釜神社 (豊橋市)}} (Aichi Prefecture, Toyohashi City) * Akahiko Shrine (Aichi Prefecture, Gamagōri City) * {{ill|Susakihama Shrine|ja|洲崎濱宮神明神社}} (Mie Prefecture, Yokkaichi City) * Kawazoe Shrine (Mie Prefecture, Ōdai Town) * Oka Shrine (Shiga Prefecture, Maibara City) * Ōta Shrine (Shiga Prefecture, Takashima City) * {{ill|Tsuno Shrine|ja|津野神社}} (Shiga Prefecture, Takashima City) * Monboku Shrine (Nara Prefecture, Soni Village) * {{ill|Mitsu Shrine|ja|御杖神社}} (Nara Prefecture, Mitsue Village) * {{ill|Jishu Shrine|ja|地主神社}} (Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City) * {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)}} (Kyoto Prefecture, Yosano Town) * {{ill|Susa Shrine|ja|須佐神社 (田辺市)}} (Wakayama Prefecture, Tanabe City) * Futamura Shrine (Hyōgo Prefecture, Tamba Sasayama City) * {{ill|Ishibe Shrine|ja|石部神社 (豊岡市)}} (Hyōgo Prefecture, Toyooka City) * Itaki Shrine (Shimane Prefecture, Yasugi City) * {{ill|Kumano Taisha|ja|熊野大社}} (Shimane Prefecture, Matsue City) * Koroku Shrine (Nagasaki Prefecture, Tsushima City) * Iwasaki Shrine (Ōita Prefecture, Usa City) * Ninomiya Shrine (Fukuoka Prefecture, Fukuoka City)

Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==

Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
