[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:生没年不詳]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}}.

From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.

[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:生没年不詳]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category People of the Kofun period
revision.text:
Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==

revision.text:
Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:Year of Birth and Death unknown]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==
* {{ill|Take Rokusho Shrine|ja|嶽六所神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take Rokusho Shrine|qq=}} (Akita Prefecture, Daisen City) * {{ill|Hanabue Shrine|ja|鼻節神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanabue Shrine|qq=}} (Miyagi Prefecture, Miyagi District) * Unagushi Shrine (Niigata Prefecture, Nagaoka City) * {{ill|Subasukiji Shrine|ja|須波阿湏疑神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunahaasu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui Prefecture, Ikeda Town) * Ugama Shrine (Fukui Prefecture, Ikeda Town) * [[:en:Tsukubasan Shrine|Tsukubasan Shrine]] (Ibaraki Prefecture, Tsukuba City) * {{ill|Kanmuri Inari Shrine|ja|冠稲荷神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanmuri Inari Shrine|qq=}} (Gunma Prefecture, Ōta Shrine|ja|洲崎濱宮神明神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susakihama Shrine|qq=}} (Mie Prefecture, Yokkaichi City) * Kawazoe Shrine (Mie Prefecture, Ōdai Town) * Oka Shrine (Shiga Prefecture, Maibara City) * Ōta Shrine (Shiga Prefecture, Takashima City) * {{ill|Tsuno Shrine|ja|津野神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuno Shrine|qq=}} (Shiga Prefecture, Takashima City) * Monboku Shrine (Nara Prefecture, Soni Village) * {{ill|Mitsue Shrine|lt=Mitsue Shrine|lt=Mitsu Shrine|ja|御杖神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsu Shrine|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture, Mitsue Prefecture, Yasugi City) * [[:en:Kumano Taisha|Kumano Taisha]] (Shimane Prefecture, Matsue City) * Koroku Shrine (Nagasaki Prefecture, Tsushima City) * Iwasaki Shrine (Ōita Prefecture, Usa City) * [[ja:太田命]] [[sn:Ōta-no-Mikoto]]
[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]]<!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories-->[[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]]

{{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|qid=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

revision.text:
{{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|qid=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]]<!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories-->[[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[en:Ōta-no-Mikoto]]

{{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|qid=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. [[ja:太田命]] [[sn:Ōta-no-Mikoto]]
[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]]<!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories-->[[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[en:Ōta-no-Mikoto]]

{{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|qid=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

{{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|qid=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

{{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|qid=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==
* {{ill|Take Rokusho Shrine|ja|嶽六所神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take Rokusho Shrine|qq=}} (Akita Prefecture, Daisen City) * {{ill|Hanabue [[ja:太田命]]
[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]]<!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories-->[[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[Category:Qq 4]]

{{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|qid=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]]<!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories-->[[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[sn:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] {{wikidata link|Q97496337}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|qid=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

[[ja:太田命]] [[sn:Ōta-no-Mikoto]]
[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]]<!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories-->[[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[sn:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] {{wikidata link|Q97496337}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.

According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okita-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okita-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.

From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.

[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]]<!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories-->[[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[sn:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] {{wikidata link|Q97496337}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

(豊岡市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishibe Shrine|qq=}} (Hyōgo Prefecture, Toyooka City) * Itaki Shrine (Shimane Prefecture, Yasugi City) * [[:en:Kumano Taisha|Kumano Taisha]] (Shimane Prefecture, <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

{{wikidata link|Q97496337}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
