[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] <!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[sn:Ōta-no-Mikoto]]
{{wikidata link|Q97496337}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] <!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[sn:Ōta-no-Mikoto]]
{{wikidata link|Q97496337}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called [[:en:Isuzu River|Isuzu River]]," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q97496337]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|en|Isuzu River|WD=Q6089522|lt=Isuzu River}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==

From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962}}}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=tutelary deity|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|en|Isuzu River|WD=Q6089522|lt=Isuzu River}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==

[[ja:大田命]] [[ja:Ōta-no-Mikoto]] <!--standardizing to enwiki standards where this would merely be the intersection of two categories--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[sn:Ōta-no-Mikoto]]
{{wikidata link|Q97496337}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:History of Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Miwa clan]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Ise Province]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962|1=Mirahime}}}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=Q98760657|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|en|Isuzu River|WD=Q6089522|lt=Isuzu River|1=Isuzu River}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:太田命]] {{Infobox person|children={{ill|Q97478962|WD=Q97478962|1=Mirahime}}}} Ōtanomikoto, also known as Okitama, is a figure mentioned in the ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', a Japanese historical text. He is described as a descendant of Sarutahiko Ōkami, the leader of the earthly kami in Shinto belief. <ref name=":0">https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/otanomikoto/</ref>
According to legend, Ōtanomikoto met Yamatohime, a princess associated with the establishment of the Ise Grand Shrine. When Yamatohime was seeking a suitable location for the shrine, Ōtanomikoto informed her about a place along the Isuzu River. He mentioned that this site featured 50 bells arranged in the shape of the shrine of Heaven, and some legends even suggest that the Jewelled Spear of Heaven was kept there. He also told her that these sacred items had been worshipped for 80,000 years. <ref name=":0" />
This encounter contributed to the selection of the site for the Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most significant Shinto shrines in Japan. The shrine is dedicated to Amaterasu Ōmikami, the sun goddess, and holds great cultural and religious importance.
Sarutahiko Ōkami, Ōtanomikoto's ancestor, is revered as a guiding deity in Shinto, symbolizing strength and guidance. He is enshrined at several locations, including the Tsubaki Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture and Sarutahiko Shrine in Ise. <ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarutahiko_%C5%8Ckami</ref>
The narrative involving Ōtanomikoto and Yamatohime highlights the deep connections between Shinto deities and the establishment of sacred sites in Japan, reflecting the intertwining of mythology and cultural heritage.
==Overview==
According to the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' (皇太神宮儀式帳), Ōta no Mikoto is described as a distant ancestor of the {{ill|Ujituchi no Kimi|ja|宇治土公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujituchi no Kimi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the ''Daijingū Shomazatsuki'' (大神宮諸雑事記), he is similarly identified as the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi no Kimi and considered a local {{ill|tutelary deity|ja|土地神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|1=Q98760657|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98760657}}.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the ''Ise Nishorōta Jingū Gochinza Denki'' (伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記) stated that "{{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja|猿田彦大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} was the distant ancestor of the Ujituchi clan," and in the ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'' (倭姫命世記), Ōta no Mikoto was claimed to be the ancestor of the Ujituchi clan, a descendant of Sarutahiko-no-Kami. From this period, Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto began to appear in genealogies as Ōta no Mikoto’s forefather.
The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|en|Isuzu River|WD=Q6089522|lt=Isuzu River|1=Isuzu River}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
== Shrines Enshrining Ōta no Mikoto ==

Shrine|qq=}} (Miyagi Prefecture, Miyagi District) * Unagushi Shrine (Niigata Prefecture, Nagaoka City) * {{ill|Subasukiji Shrine|ja|須波阿湏疑神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sunahaasu Shrine|qq=}} (Fukui The ''Jinmei Hisho'' (神名秘書) and other texts identify Ōta no Mikoto as another name for {{ill|Okitama-no-Kami|ja|興玉神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitama-no-Kami|qq=}}.
The Hamanasoja Shinmei-no-Miya is considered a candidate for the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} Eita Shrine and enshrines Ōta no Mikoto as the ancestral deity of the Hamananokenushi.
The ''Kōtai Jingū Gishikichō'' describes how {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} asked Ōta no Mikoto about the name of his land, to which he replied, "This river is called {{ill|Isuzu River|en|Isuzu River|WD=Q6089522|lt=Isuzu River|1=Isuzu River}}," and indicated a suitable site for a grand shrine upriver.
